---
# PAPER GENRE: Empirical Health Behavior / Social Determinants Study
# JOURNAL: Journal of Health and Social Behavior
#
# JHSB is a sociology journal — not an epidemiology journal. Papers must
# advance sociological THEORY about health, not just report associations.
# The theoretical contribution is the paper's primary value proposition.
#
# GENRE CONTEXT (from catademic classification of 134 JHSB papers, 2023–2026):
#   41.8% — Racial/ethnic health disparities and structural racism
#   23.1% — Education and health outcomes
#   16.4% — Primarily theoretical or conceptual
#   14.2% — American politics, governance, or policy
#    9.0% — COVID-19 pandemic impacts
#   38.1% — Other (immigration, family structure, mental health, medical sociology)
#
# ABSTRACT STRUCTURE (derived from sentence-level coding of 5 JHSB papers):
#   6 moves in ~8-10 sentences. Theory is always named, not left implicit.
#   Gap is framed as a conceptual limitation, not "no one has studied X."
#   Findings use sociological language, not just statistical results.
#
title: "[Social Factor] and [Health Outcome]: [Specification]"
author:
  - name: [Author Name]
    affiliation: [Institution]
    email: [email]
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Abstract

<!-- 8-10 sentences total, 6 moves:
1. Field positioning (1-2 sentences)
2. Gap / reframe (1-2 sentences) — conceptual, not just "no one has studied X"
3. This paper (1 sentence) — active verb: explores, examines, asks
4. Data (1 sentence) — method, sample, scope in one sentence
5. Findings (2-3 sentences) — sociological language, not just statistics
6. So-what (1 sentence) — theoretical significance, not just policy
-->


# Introduction

<!-- ~4 paragraphs. Funnels from broad to specific. Findings previewed — no suspense. -->

<!-- P1: Problem + stakes.
Establish the societal problem with empirical evidence. Make it concrete
and timely. -->

<!-- P2: Literature → gap.
Review what the field knows, then pivot to what it doesn't. The gap is
a conceptual failure, not just absence — "existing work overlooks" or
"reduces this to X." -->

<!-- P3: This paper + theoretical positioning.
What this study does AND why the dominant approach is insufficient.
Name the alternative lens. State the method. -->

<!-- P4: Findings preview + significance.
Give away the answer. State the theoretical contribution. Final sentence
generalizes beyond the specific case. -->


# Background

<!-- The funnel isn't just Introduction → Background. It operates WITHIN
Background too. The subsections move from WHAT → WHAT SPECIFICALLY → WHY:
  Topic 1: The broad phenomenon (empirical, ~3 paragraphs)
  Topic 2: The specific driver (empirical, ~3 paragraphs)
  Topic 3: The theoretical framework (theory, ~5 paragraphs — longest, comes last)
Each subsection narrows from the previous one. Topics 1-2 are empirical
reviews; Topic 3 is where the paper stakes its intellectual claim. The theory
subsection is the destination the funnel has been building toward. -->

## [Topic 1: The Phenomenon]

<!-- ~3 paragraphs per subsection. -->

<!-- P1: Define + frame.
Define the phenomenon. Name the field's dominant framework. Acknowledge its
influence, then pivot: "However, research has not fully explored..." Connect
to your theoretical terrain. -->

<!-- P2: Evidence review.
Survey empirical literature across populations/contexts. Introduce bridging
concepts that link existing work to your angle. Synthesize at the end. -->

<!-- P3: Bridge to this study.
"This study builds on this body of work by..." Name what the dominant
approach misses. State the methodological or conceptual contribution. -->

## [Topic 2: The Specific Driver / Mechanism]

<!-- Narrows from the broad phenomenon to the specific angle this paper
investigates. Traces the causal or historical pathway — not just "studies
found X" but how the mechanism developed over time. Each citation adds a
link in the chain. Ends with a more specific bridge to this study than
Topic 1 did. -->

<!-- P1: Establish the specific angle + show it predates the current moment +
identify what's still missing. -->

<!-- P2: Trace the causal/historical pathway. Walk through the mechanism:
how did we get here? Each citation adds a link in the chain. -->

<!-- P3: Synthesize + restate the gap (now more specific) + position this study. -->

## [Topic 3: The Theoretical Framework]

<!-- The deepest part of the funnel. Longer than the empirical subsections
(~5 paragraphs). This is where the paper plants its theoretical flag. -->

<!-- P1: Name the framework + core insights.
Name the approach and its intellectual lineage. Acknowledge existing work,
then identify what it leaves implicit. Deliver the framework's core insights
(can number them). -->

<!-- P2: Apply the framework to the paper's domain.
Redefine key concepts through the framework's lens. State what it explains
for this topic. Introduce the paper's specific theoretical constructs. -->

<!-- P3: Define construct 1.
Formal definition. Historical grounding. Pivot to the present — how current
conditions (e.g., polarization) disrupt or activate it. Key claim: the
construct is relational / contingent / mediated by X. -->

<!-- P4: Define construct 2.
Formal definition. How it operates as a cognitive mechanism. How it applies
to the paper's domain. How current conditions reinforce it. -->

<!-- P5: Synthesize.
"Taken together..." State what the combined framework reveals. Reframe the
debate: it's not about X, it's about Y. This is the theoretical punchline —
the claim the rest of the paper tests. -->

## The Current Study

<!-- ~3 paragraphs. Translates the theoretical framework into bounded,
testable claims. More modest in tone than the theory section. -->

<!-- P1: Link theory to testable claims.
Restate the central mechanism from Topic 3. Name the specific measures
that capture the constructs. Deliver the primary hypothesis. -->

<!-- P2: Introduce variation / moderation.
What conditions might alter the main relationship? Explain the mechanism.
Deliver secondary hypothesis(es). -->

<!-- P3: Scope the contribution.
What this study provides (empirical strategy, new measures, etc.) and
what it does NOT claim to do. Ground the contribution in the specific
context. -->


# Data and Methods

## Data

<!-- ~3 paragraphs + instrument table. -->

<!-- P1: Source + design + sample.
Name the survey, funder, who administered it, sampling frame. Note design
strengths relevant to this study. State full N → analytic N with reason
for any restriction. -->

<!-- [Table: Survey instrument / vignette wording placed here if applicable.
Reader sees exactly what was measured before prose continues.] -->

<!-- P2: Representativeness.
Compare sample to national benchmarks. Acknowledge deviations and explain
why acceptable. State how deviations are handled (controls, weights). -->

<!-- P3: Missing data strategy.
Name the technique, justify briefly. One paragraph. -->

<!-- P4: Survey design details (~1 paragraph).
How items were administered — randomization, embedding within broader
modules, strategies to minimize response bias / priming. -->

<!-- P5: Data limitations (~1 paragraph).
Enumerate specific limitations (nonresponse bias, sample deviations,
data age, etc.). State how each is mitigated. End with call for future
replication if applicable. Note: data limitations can appear here in
Methods rather than only in Discussion. -->

## Measures

### Dependent Variable(s)

<!-- ~3 paragraphs. -->

<!-- P1: Introduce what's measured + framing.
State the constructs being captured. Present vignette or framing text
verbatim if applicable. -->

<!-- P2: List specific items.
Name each dimension / indicator. Provide exact item wording. -->

<!-- P3: Coding + validation.
How responses were coded (direction, scaling). Validation technique
(CFA, factor loadings, alpha, etc.). Reference tables for full wording
and descriptive frequencies. -->

### Key Independent Variable(s)

<!-- ~1 paragraph. More compact than DVs. Name the items, give scale details,
validate (CFA, alpha, etc.), and state where it enters the model. -->

### Control Variables

<!-- ~1 paragraph. Front-load the most theoretically relevant control and
give it 2-3 sentences (how measured, scored, scaled). Then list remaining
demographic/contextual controls efficiently with coding details. Reference
appendix for descriptive statistics. Close with a sentence justifying the
set as a whole. -->

## Analytical Strategy

<!-- Longest Methods subsection (~6 paragraphs). Every modeling choice is
tied back to theory, not just stated as a technical decision. Each
hypothesis gets its own model paragraph. -->

<!-- P1: Roadmap.
"The analysis proceeded in N stages." Name each stage briefly. -->

<!-- P2: Justify the method.
Why this technique fits this theory. What it does technically. What
problems it avoids vs. alternatives. -->

<!-- P3: Formal specification.
Equations or model structure. Define terms. State estimation method
with justification (why it fits the data). -->

<!-- P4: Model for H1.
Describe model constraints that operationalize H1. What the specification
assumes. Reference a conceptual figure. Acknowledge limitations of the
specification. -->

<!-- P5: Model for H2 (or alternative specification).
How it relaxes/extends the H1 model. Name each modification with
theoretical justification. Include interaction terms if applicable. -->

<!-- P6: Model fit.
Name fit statistics, state thresholds, report verdict. -->


# Results

<!-- ~8 paragraphs of prose + tables. No subsection headers — results are
presented as a single continuous section.

KEY PRINCIPLES (derived from paragraph-level coding):
- Structure mirrors the hypotheses: test H1, test H2, extend, confirm.
- Every paragraph has exactly one job.
- Open with credibility: validate measurement BEFORE presenting substance.
  The reader must trust the instruments before they'll trust the results.
- Orient the reader: teach them how to read the table, walk through the
  least controversial column first, earn trust on the easy stuff before
  the main results.
- Lead each hypothesis paragraph with the verdict, then show the evidence.
  Name the hypothesis explicitly ("supports H1") — no ambiguity.
- Report interactions substantively, not just statistically. Show the math
  (combined effect at +1 SD) and connect to prior research.
- Compare magnitudes across predictors — this is the interpretive move
  that makes coefficients meaningful ("as large as political orientation
  but in the opposite direction").
- Simpler hypotheses get shorter paragraphs. Don't pad.
- Extend beyond the hypotheses to show relational/contrasting patterns.
  The paper doesn't just test its claims, it shows the broader landscape.
- Non-findings are interpreted, not hidden. Explain WHY the mechanism
  doesn't apply in a given case.
- Close with model comparison as a final confirmation device — not just
  "our model fits" but "our model fits better than alternatives." Tie
  each comparison back to a specific hypothesis.
- Arc: validate → orient → H1 → H2 → extend → extend → confirm. -->

<!-- P1: Validate measurement.
Present CFA / factor loadings. Note stability across specifications.
Establish that measurement is sound before moving to substance. -->

<!-- [Table: CFA / factor loadings] -->

<!-- P2: Name the constructs + validate key IV.
Define what the latent variables capture (give them a collective label).
Apply same validation to the independent variable. Note supplementary
robustness checks. -->

<!-- P3: Model fit + covariates model.
Report overall model fit (RMSEA, CFI, etc.). Walk through the "baseline"
column — what predicts the key IV. Flag surprising findings. -->

<!-- [Table: Full structural model / regression results] -->

<!-- P4: H1 test — primary outcome.
State whether hypothesis is supported. Report the key coefficient +
interaction if applicable. Connect to prior research. Note indirect
effects. -->

<!-- P5: H2 test — secondary mechanism.
Report direct effects supporting H2. Compare magnitude to the primary
predictor. State the substantive interpretation. -->

<!-- P6: Extend the logic — additional outcome(s).
Examine secondary outcomes to show relational or contrasting patterns.
How does the primary predictor operate differently here? Theoretical
interpretation. -->

<!-- P7: Further extension — remaining outcome(s).
If a third outcome reveals different drivers, interpret why. Reinforce
the theoretical framework. Note non-findings explicitly. -->

<!-- P8: Model comparison / robustness.
Compare full model to alternative specifications. Which constraints
improve or worsen fit? Final verdict: results substantiate both
hypotheses. -->

<!-- [Table: Alternative model comparisons] -->


# Discussion

<!-- ~5 paragraphs. No subsection headers — presented as continuous prose.
Opens by situating findings in intellectual tradition, closes by zooming
out to broadest significance. Limitations are integrated into P3, not
quarantined in their own section. -->

<!-- P1: Historical/theoretical anchor + core finding.
Open with a canonical citation or foundational idea that frames the
findings in a broader sociological lineage. Connect that tradition to
the present moment. Restate the core finding. Reframe the phenomenon —
it's not about X, it's about Y. -->

<!-- P2: Findings summary + mechanism + intervention point.
Restate key results in conceptual language (not coefficients). Name the
surprising finding. Name the counterweight or moderating force. Final
sentence identifies a practical implication — grounded in findings, not
generic. -->

<!-- P3: Theoretical contribution + practical implications + limitations.
State the contribution to the field explicitly. Name what the framework
adds. Pivot to practical implications. Then limitations — scoped honestly.
Reframe limitations as strengths where possible (enduring patterns, tools
that can be extended). -->

<!-- P4: Future directions.
Each direction tied to a specific limitation or finding:
  - Longitudinal (addresses cross-sectional limitation)
  - Comparative (addresses single-context limitation)
  - Other domains (addresses generalizability)
  - Experimental (addresses causal claims) -->

<!-- P5: Closing.
Zoom out beyond the specific study. State what KIND of understanding is
needed (sociological, not just technical). Final sentence is forward-looking
and normative — what the field or society needs. -->


# References

::: {#refs}
:::
